entries.name: Las Leñas
entries.desc: A remote resort in the Andes of Mendoza province, Argentina, operating in the Southern Hemisphere winter. It is known among advanced skiers for extensive steep and off-piste terrain.

photo.alt: Snow-covered Andes cordillera peaks at Las Leñas
photo.credit: <!-- photo: las-lenas-cordillera.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Las Leñas Cordillera.jpg" by Calamarcito · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Las_Le%C3%B1as_Cordillera.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Anton am Arlberg
entries.desc: An Austrian village in the Tyrol and part of the Ski Arlberg area, one of the largest linked systems in the country. It is known for demanding terrain, deep snow, and a long-standing après-ski culture.

photo.alt: St. Anton am Arlberg village along the river, backed by Tyrolean peaks
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-anton-am-arlberg-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "St. Anton am Arlberg.jpg" by Steffen Mokosch · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:St._Anton_am_Arlberg.jpg -->

entries.name: Kitzbühel
entries.desc: A medieval walled town in the Austrian Tyrol. It hosts the Hahnenkamm downhill race on the Streif, one of the most demanding courses on the Alpine World Cup circuit.

photo.alt: View over Kitzbühel and its lakes from the Hahnenkamm
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kitzbuhel-hahnenkamm.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Kitzbühel Hahnenkamm.jpg" by Lada89 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kitzb%C3%BChel_Hahnenkamm.jpg -->

entries.name: Ischgl
entries.desc: A high-altitude Austrian resort whose Silvretta Arena links across the border to Samnaun in Switzerland. It is known for a long, snow-sure season and a prominent nightlife and concert scene.

photo.alt: Groomed pistes and lift towers in the Silvretta Arena above Ischgl
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ischgl-pists.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Ischgl pists.jpg" by Gürkan Sengün · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ischgl_pists.jpg -->

entries.name: Whistler Blackcomb
entries.desc: Two connected mountains in British Columbia's Coast Range, linked by the Peak 2 Peak Gondola. Among the largest ski areas in North America, it hosted Alpine events for the 2010 Winter Olympics.

photo.alt: Whistler Mountain's snow-streaked upper slopes with a chairlift line
photo.credit: <!-- photo: whistler-mountain.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Whistler Skilift 040.jpg" by Waqcku at English Wikipedia · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Whistler_Skilift_040.jpg -->

entries.name: Lake Louise
entries.desc: A large ski area within Banff National Park in the Canadian Rockies, set among glaciers and peaks. It regularly hosts the opening speed races of the Alpine World Cup season.

photo.alt: Lake Louise's turquoise water framed by glaciers and peaks in Banff National Park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lake-louise-banff.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Lake Louis, Banff National Park, Canadian Rockies.jpg" by Preeteesh · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lake_Louis,_Banff_National_Park,_Canadian_Rockies.jpg -->

entries.name: Mont Tremblant
entries.desc: A resort in the Laurentian Mountains of Quebec and one of the leading ski destinations in eastern Canada. It is known for its pedestrian village built in traditional French-Canadian style.

photo.alt: Snowy street in the pedestrian village at Mont Tremblant, Quebec
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mont-tremblant-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Mont-Tremblant Village.JPG" by P199 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mont-Tremblant_Village.JPG -->

entries.name: Valle Nevado
entries.desc: A high-altitude resort in the Andes within reach of Santiago, running during the Southern Hemisphere winter. It connects to neighboring areas to form one of South America's largest ski domains.

photo.alt: Sunny ski slope and lift line at Valle Nevado in the Andes above Santiago
photo.credit: <!-- photo: valle-nevado-andes.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Vale Nevado - Chile (14669632357).jpg" by Cesar I. Martins from Jundiai, Brazil · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vale_Nevado_-_Chile_(14669632357).jpg -->

entries.name: Val Thorens
entries.desc: The highest ski resort in Europe, purpose-built above the treeline in the French Alps. It anchors Les 3 Vallées, the world's largest linked ski area, and is known for snow-sure high-altitude terrain.

photo.alt: Val Thorens ski village above the treeline in the French Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: val-thorens-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Val Thorens, vue du nord (2016).JPG" by Florian Pépellin · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Val_Thorens,_vue_du_nord_(2016).JPG -->

entries.name: Courchevel
entries.desc: A cluster of villages in the French Alps forming part of Les 3 Vallées. Long associated with luxury tourism, it offers wide groomed pistes and terrain suited to a range of ability levels.

photo.alt: Courchevel 1850, the highest village in the Courchevel resort cluster
photo.credit: <!-- photo: courchevel-1850-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Courchevel 1850.JPG" by Nikgrech · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Courchevel_1850.JPG -->

entries.name: Val d'Isère – Tignes
entries.desc: Two linked French Alpine resorts sharing the Espace Killy ski area. Known for a long season, high-altitude glacier skiing, and a history of hosting Alpine World Cup and Olympic races.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Val d'Isère in the French Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: val-disere-aerial-view.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Val d'Isere vue aerienne.jpg" by Mmaconta · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Val_d%27Isere_vue_aerienne.jpg -->

entries.name: Chamonix Mont-Blanc
entries.desc: A historic town at the foot of Mont Blanc that hosted the first Winter Olympics in 1924. It is a mountaineering hub known for steep, challenging terrain including the Vallée Blanche off-piste descent.

photo.alt: Chamonix valley beneath Mont Blanc in the French Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chamonix-mont-blanc-valley.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Chamonix valley and Mont-Blanc viewed from Grand Montets, Argentiere - panoramio.jpg" by arnepetter · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chamonix_valley_and_Mont-Blanc_viewed_from_Grand_Montets,_Argentiere_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: Cortina d'Ampezzo
entries.desc: An Italian resort town set among the dramatic peaks of the Dolomites. It hosted the 1956 Winter Olympics and is a co-host of the 2026 Games, and connects to the wider Dolomiti Superski network.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Cortina d'Ampezzo in the Dolomites
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cortina-dampezzo-aerial-view.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Cortina dall'alto.jpg" by Snowdog · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cortina_dall%27alto.jpg -->

entries.name: Selva Val Gardena
entries.desc: A village in the Val Gardena of South Tyrol, part of the vast Dolomiti Superski area. It sits along the Sellaronda circuit, a full-day tour around the Sella massif on linked lifts and pistes.

photo.alt: Val Gardena village beneath the Sella massif in the Dolomites
photo.credit: <!-- photo: selva-val-gardena-sella-massif.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Val Gardena with Sella and Saslonch from Resciesa.JPG" by Wolfgang Moroder · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Val_Gardena_with_Sella_and_Saslonch_from_Resciesa.JPG -->

entries.name: Livigno
entries.desc: A high-altitude Alpine town in Lombardy near the Swiss border, holding duty-free status. Its snow-sure elevation and sunny terrain have made it a training base and a venue for the 2026 Winter Olympics.

photo.alt: Livigno, a high-altitude Alpine town in Lombardy
photo.credit: <!-- photo: livigno-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Livigno - panoramio (25) retouched.jpg" by qwesy qwesy · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Livigno_-_panoramio_(25)_retouched.jpg -->

entries.name: Niseko
entries.desc: A resort area on Japan's northern island of Hokkaido, world-renowned for consistent, light powder snow driven by Siberian weather systems. It draws a large international, particularly Australian, following.

photo.alt: Mount Yōtei viewed from the Niseko ski area in Hokkaido
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mount-yotei-niseko.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Mount Yōtei from Niseko Annupuri (33253188670).jpg" by MIKI Yoshihito · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mount_Y%C5%8Dtei_from_Niseko_Annupuri_(33253188670).jpg -->

entries.name: Hakuba Valley
entries.desc: A valley in Japan's Northern Alps grouping several linked resorts. It hosted downhill, jumping, and Nordic events for the 1998 Nagano Winter Olympics and is known for steep terrain and heavy snowfall.

photo.alt: Snow-covered ski runs across the Hakuba Valley peaks in Nagano's Northern Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hakuba-happo-one-resort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Hakuba Happo-One Snow Resort" by Raita Futo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hakuba_Happo-One_Snow_Resort_(51846678465).jpg -->

entries.name: Nozawa Onsen
entries.desc: A traditional hot-spring village in Nagano that combines a ski resort with historic public bathhouses. It preserves an old-Japan atmosphere and is known for its annual fire festival.

photo.alt: Ski runs cut into the mountain above Nozawa Onsen village, Nagano
photo.credit: <!-- photo: nozawa-onsen-ski-slopes.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Nozawa Onsen 01" by Hideyuki KAMON · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Nozawa_Onsen_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Furano
entries.desc: A resort in central Hokkaido known for dry powder snow and a quieter, more local feel than the island's larger destinations. It has hosted Alpine World Cup races and sits near lavender-field farmland.

photo.alt: Ski runs on the mountains above Furano, with the snow-covered valley below
photo.credit: <!-- photo: furano-snow-resort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Furano Snow Resort view1" by wakimasa · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Furano_Snow_Resort_view1.JPG -->

entries.name: Queenstown (Remarkables + Coronet Peak)
entries.desc: A lakeside resort town on New Zealand's South Island serving two nearby ski fields, The Remarkables and Coronet Peak. It runs in the Southern Hemisphere winter amid dramatic mountain scenery.

photo.alt: View over the Coronet Peak ski fields toward Queenstown with cloud filling the valley
photo.credit: <!-- photo: coronet-peak-ski-fields.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Coronet Peak Ski Fields - panoramio" by Bart Römgens · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Coronet_Peak_Ski_Fields_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: Treble Cone
entries.desc: The largest ski area in New Zealand's South Island, above Lake Wanaka. It is known for long runs, off-piste chutes, and expansive views over the surrounding mountains and lake.

photo.alt: Backcountry skiers climbing a ridge above Treble Cone's off-piste terrain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: treble-cone-summit.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Trebleconesummit" by Maelgwn · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Trebleconesummit.jpg -->

entries.name: Lofoten
entries.desc: An Arctic archipelago in northern Norway where mountains rise straight from the sea. Rather than a lift-served resort, it is a ski-touring destination combining backcountry descents with coastal scenery.

photo.alt: A snow-covered fishing village on the frozen shore beneath Lofoten's peaks
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lofoten-fishing-village-winter.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Ballstad-winter" by Algkalv · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ballstad-winter.jpg -->

entries.name: Åre
entries.desc: The largest Alpine ski resort in Sweden, set on a lakeside mountain in Jämtland. It has hosted Alpine World Championships and is one of the leading winter destinations in Scandinavia.

photo.alt: Ice-coated chairlift towers rise above the clouds on Åreskutan mountain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: areskutan-ski-lift.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Snowy Åreskutan Ski lift" by Anders Carlsson · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Snowy_Åreskutan_Ski_lift.jpg -->

entries.name: Zermatt
entries.desc: A car-free Swiss village beneath the Matterhorn in the Valais Alps. Its high-altitude glacier terrain links to Cervinia in Italy and supports skiing across much of the year.

photo.alt: The Matterhorn rising above the valley near Zermatt
photo.credit: <!-- photo: matterhorn-from-zermatt.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Matterhorn Zermatt" by Pearlnight · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Matterhorn_Zermatt.JPG -->

entries.name: Verbier
entries.desc: A Swiss resort in the Valais and the main gateway to the 4 Vallées ski area. It is widely regarded as a freeride destination and has hosted stops on the Freeride World Tour.

photo.alt: Verbier village and ski lift line in the Swiss Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: verbier-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Verbier Valais 029.JPG" by Norbert Aepli · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Verbier_Valais_029.JPG -->

entries.name: St. Moritz
entries.desc: A resort town in the Engadin valley of eastern Switzerland and one of the birthplaces of Alpine winter tourism. It has hosted the Winter Olympics twice and is known for its lake setting and sunny climate.

photo.alt: Lake St. Moritz framed by the Engadin mountains
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lake-st-moritz.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Sankt Moritz Lake Piz Muragl.jpg" by Isiwal · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sankt_Moritz_Lake_Piz_Muragl.jpg -->

entries.name: Aspen Snowmass
entries.desc: A Colorado destination spanning four mountains: Aspen Mountain, Aspen Highlands, Buttermilk, and Snowmass. A former silver-mining town, it hosts the Winter X Games at Buttermilk each year.

photo.alt: Silver Queen Gondola and chairlift line on Aspen Mountain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: aspen-mountain-gondola.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Aspen Mountain Silver Queen Gondola.jpg" by Wolfgang Moroder · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aspen_Mountain_Silver_Queen_Gondola.jpg -->

entries.name: Vail
entries.desc: One of the largest single ski mountains in North America, in the Colorado Rockies. It is known for its expansive Back Bowls and the pedestrian village modeled on Alpine towns.

photo.alt: Pedestrian village street in Vail with the Gore Range behind
photo.credit: <!-- photo: vail-village-gore-creek-drive.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Gore Creek Drive - Vail, CO.jpg" by Nick Csakany · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gore_Creek_Drive_-_Vail,_CO.jpg -->

meta: One of Largest Single Mountains in N. America

entries.name: Jackson Hole
entries.desc: A Wyoming resort in the Teton Range known for steep, rugged terrain and a large vertical drop. Its aerial tram and the Corbet's Couloir chute have given it a reputation for expert-level skiing.

photo.alt: Corbet's Couloir chute beneath the aerial tram cable at Jackson Hole
photo.credit: <!-- photo: corbets-couloir-jackson-hole.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Corbet's Couloir jackson hole.jpg" by Enricokamasa · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Corbet%27s_Couloir_jackson_hole.jpg -->

entries.name: Park City
entries.desc: The largest ski resort in the United States, in Utah's Wasatch Range near Salt Lake City. It hosted events for the 2002 Winter Olympics and is home to the Sundance Film Festival.

photo.alt: Historic Main Street storefronts in Park City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: park-city-main-street.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "View on Main Street in Park City Utah.JPG" by Saalebaer · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:View_on_Main_Street_in_Park_City_Utah.JPG -->
